document: Second: External Environment Analysis of the Faculty
institution: Nile University — Faculty of Information Technology and Computer Science
methodology_note: The work team conducted the analysis by applying the PESTEL methodology (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental & Legal), which is considered one of the most well-known methods for analyzing external-environment factors affecting an institution's performance. This method classifies these factors into: political, economic, social, technological, environmental, and legal factors. PESTEL analysis helps provide a clear picture of the external factors surrounding the institution, which can be used in selecting the most suitable strategic options.
opportunities: Recognition by international institutions of the high quality of the Faculty's academic standards, which gives its graduates a strong international reputation
The rapid development in computing and the underlying infrastructure for information technology, and securing it
The increasing number of multidisciplinary local and international projects relying on information-technology techniques and digital transformation
The close link between the growth and development of various sectors and the information-technology industry
The growing need for highly qualified information-technology specialists
The existence of numerous local and foreign funding bodies
The existence of a Quality Assurance and Accreditation Authority that supports accreditation efforts
The proximity of the geographic location to multiple information-technology and knowledge centers, such as the Smart Village
The diversity of emerging information-technology specializations that enjoy strong support locally, regionally, and internationally
The increasing demand for education and scientific research in the information-technology, computer-science, and artificial-intelligence sector
threats: Existence of international, regional, and local competition at both the academic level and the information-technology industry level to employ better-qualified young people
Increased competition in attracting distinguished students due to the establishment of a number of faculties with similar specializations at other universities
The strong pull of international institutions on our expatriate scientists, which affects the availability of qualified faculty members and researchers
The risk of losing talented faculty members due to available opportunities at competing universities
Changing labor-market needs due to the rapid development in the field of information technology
The university's location on a highway, which puts students at risk
A growing trend among a segment of parents and students toward studying abroad
